summary: Julia Evans documents a frustrating day spent debugging a linker problem in her OS project. She and a pair programming partner discovered that ELF files only work when sections are ordered in a specific way (.text .rodata .data .bss), but she can't figure out why. Despite fixing the issue, the lack of understanding is clearly bothering her. She posted a StackOverflow question that went unanswered and began writing a tutorial on binary formats as a consolation project.
key_points: ELF section ordering (.text .rodata .data .bss) matters for correctness, but the reason why is not obvious even after fixing the bug
Pair programming helped discover the fix through experimentation, even without full understanding of the root cause
Some low-level systems topics like linker scripts have sparse community support, even on StackOverflow
Writing tutorials and explanations can be a productive response to frustration with a problem
money_quotes: I've fixed it, but I don't understand *why* it's fixed, and I am having no luck.
Today I spent pretty much the whole day trying to figure out what's going on with a linker problem I'm having.
I also created a StackOverflow question but it is not getting any love.
key_insight: Fixing a bug without understanding why it's fixed is deeply unsatisfying, and linker internals are one of those areas where even community resources fall short.
